agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Changes to Burnaby's 2026 Council meeting schedule
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on proposed changes to the schedule of regular council meetings for 2026. The proposal, outlined in a memorandum on the agenda, would adjust the dates and possibly the frequency of meetings next year. This is not yet approved — council will consider and decide on the new schedule at an upcoming meeting. Business owners who regularly attend or monitor council meetings should be aware that the dates may shift.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on the 2026 meeting schedule — it could affect when you can attend or submit input.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

1.	Changes to the 2026 Council Meetings Schedule

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Commemoration Framework report up for council review
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a staff report on a proposed Commemoration Framework for the City of Vancouver. This framework would set new rules for how the city decides what to commemorate — for example, naming parks, streets, or buildings, or putting up statues and plaques. The goal is to make sure commemorations reflect the city's diverse history and values. The report is on the agenda for discussion, but no final decision has been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby development property tax relief program for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Development Potential Relief Program for 2026 property taxation. This program would allow owners of land held for future development to apply for a reduction in their property taxes, based on the land's current use rather than its highest potential value. If approved, the bylaw would lower the tax burden on properties that are being held for development but are not yet generating income from that development. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — if approved, you may need to apply for the tax relief before a deadline.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Property Taxation: Development Potential Relief Program By-law, Tax Rating      By-laws, and Averaging Resolutions

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby capital budget adjustments for May 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed adjustments to the City's capital budget for May 2026. These adjustments would reallocate funds for various city infrastructure projects, such as road repairs, park upgrades, and building maintenance. If approved, the changes could affect contracts and timelines for businesses working on city projects. The decision is scheduled for the next council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, check if your business has city contracts that may be affected by the budget changes.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Operator and lease approval for social facility at 485 West 42nd Ave
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on appointing an operator and approving a lease for a social facility at #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue. Recommendations B, C and D require at least 2/3 of all council members to vote 'yes' under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule that needs a supermajority for certain decisions). agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Closure and sale or lease of lane near 1750 Franklin Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to close, sell, or lease a public lane that runs next to properties at 1750 Franklin Street and 1717-1771 East Hastings Street. If approved, this would transfer ownership or control of that lane to the adjacent property owners. This could affect access, parking, or future development plans for nearby businesses and residents. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw changes for inclusionary zoning and density bonus
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on amendments to the Development Approval Procedure Bylaw. The proposed changes would add new consultation requirements for two housing programs: Inclusionary Zoning (which requires new developments to include affordable housing units) and Density Bonus (which lets developers build more units in exchange for community benefits like parks or childcare). If approved, developers would need to follow new steps for public consultation before their projects can move forward. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, these new consultation rules will affect how you apply for development permits in Burnaby.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for mixed-use at 185-193 SW Marine Drive and 7980 Manitoba Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would change the zoning rules for two properties at 185-193 Southwest Marine Drive and 7980 Manitoba Street. The amendment would allow a new mixed-use development — meaning a building that combines both commercial and residential uses — on these sites. This is a text amendment to the existing Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone, which is a special zoning category that allows the city to set custom rules for a specific property. The decision has not been made yet; Council will consider the draft bylaw at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby density bonus and inclusionary zoning updates for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed updates to Burnaby's density bonus and inclusionary zoning rules. A density bonus lets developers build more units than normally allowed in exchange for community benefits like affordable housing. Inclusionary zoning requires new developments to include a certain number of affordable homes. These updates are meant to bring the city's rules in line with new provincial legislation (Bill 16). If approved, the changes could affect how much developers pay for density bonuses and how many affordable units they must include in new projects. This is a proposal only — nothing has been decided yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — these changes could affect your development costs or project requirements.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Vancouver's Commemoration Framework and delegated plaque approvals
minutes_briefs.summary: Council approved the City of Vancouver Commemoration Framework, a set of rules for deciding what people, events, or places get officially remembered by the city. Council also gave city staff the authority to approve or reject applications for temporary plaques that will be displayed for up to 10 years, as long as the applications meet the new criteria. This decision is final and staff will create guidelines and public resources by the end of 2026.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
1. City of Vancouver Commemoration Framework
March 17, 2026

Staff from Arts, Culture and Community Services provided a presentation and responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Kirby-Yung
SECONDED by Councillor Dominato
A. THAT Council approve the City of Vancouver Commemoration Framework ("Framework") attached as Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled "City of Vancouver Commemoration Framework", and direct staff to develop internal guidelines and public-facing resources to implement the Framework by Q4 2026.
B. THAT Council delegate the following authorities to City staff:

i. the General Manager of the lead Department (typically the Department that owns the applicable asset) may decline Applications where the Assessment Criteria (as defined in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled "City of Vancouver Commemoration Framework") are not adequately met, and  
ii. the General Manager of the lead Department (typically the Department that owns the applicable asset) may approve Applications (as defined in Appendix A of the above-noted report) for fully funded, temporary plaques that will be displayed for up to 10 years and that demonstrate a strong alignment to the Assessment Criteria.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11564)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 2026 property tax relief bylaw and tax rates for Burnaby
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved the 2026 Development Potential Tax Relief By-law, which provides property tax relief for landowners whose land's development potential has been restricted. Council also adopted the 2026 General Purpose Tax Rating By-law and the 2026 Metro Vancouver Regional District Rating By-law, which set the municipal and regional tax rates for the year. Additionally, Council approved Averaging Resolutions that adjust tax rates from other authorities to support the City's land assessment averaging program. These decisions finalize the property tax framework for 2026.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax assessment to see if you qualify for the Development Potential Tax Relief program.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $27M increase to Burnaby's 2023-2026 Capital Plan
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved a $27 million increase to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan and a $63 million increase to Multi-Year Capital Project Budgets. This decision, made on April 21, 2026, adjusts the city's long-term spending plan for major infrastructure and equipment projects. The capital plan covers big-ticket items like roads, parks, buildings, and utility systems. The additional funding will be allocated to various city projects already in the pipeline.
minutes_briefs.source_body:

April 21, 2026

Staff from Finance and Supply Chain Management and Engineering Services responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Kirby-Yung
SECONDED by Councillor Dominato

THAT Council approve adjustments to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan and the Multi-Year Capital Project Budgets as outlined in the Report dated April 21, 2026, entitled "Quarterly Capital Budget Adjustments – May 2026", and Appendices A and B of the same report, which will result in:

a. An increase of $27 million to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan; and
b. An increase of $63 million to Multi-Year Capital Project Budgets.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Disability Alliance BC as social facility operator and lease
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved appointing Disability Alliance BC Society (DABC) as the non-profit operator and lead tenant of a new City-owned social facility at #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue. Council also authorized a 15-year lease (initial 5-year term with two 5-year renewal options) at a base rent of $10 per term, which is well below market rate. This low rent, plus waived capital maintenance costs, represents an in-kind grant worth about $667,000 per year (over $3.35 million for the first 5 years) and an additional $172,550 per year in waived maintenance costs (over $862,750 for the first 5 years). The decision is final, and the lease will be negotiated and executed by City staff.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No action required from small business owners — this decision directly affects only the City and Disability Alliance BC.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
4. Operator Appointment and Lease Approval for Social Facility at #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue
April 7, 2026

A. THAT Council appoint Disability Alliance BC Society ("DABC") as the non-profit operator and lead tenant of a new social facility to be owned by the City, with a civic address of #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue, and a legal description of: Parcel Identifier: 032-742-789, Air Space Parcel 4 Block 857 District Lot 526 Group 1 New Westminster District Air Space Plan EPP146681 (the "Premises").
B. THAT Council delegate its authority to the Director of Real Estate Services to negotiate and execute a lease agreement with DABC for the Premises (the "Lease"), on the following basic terms and as further set out in Appendix A of the Report dated April 7, 2026, entitled "Operator Appointment and Lease Approval for Social Facility at #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue", and upon such other terms and conditions satisfactory to the General Manager of Real Estate, Environment and Facilities Management, the General Manager of Arts, Culture and Community Services, and the Director of Legal Services;
Term and Renewals: Five (5) years initial with two (2) options to renew, each for a further five (5) years for a maximum term of fifteen (15) years;

Each renewal option is conditional upon the City being satisfied that DABC has the capacity to operate, maintain and program the Premises in accordance with the Lease terms and public service requirements for the duration of the renewal term contemplated; and  

Base Rent: Ten dollars ($10.00) for each term or renewal term, inclusive of payment of rent in lieu of property taxes, payable in advance.  
C. THAT as the base rent for the Premises will be below the applicable market rate and includes payment of rent in lieu of property taxes, B above constitutes approval of an in-kind grant estimated at approximately $667,000 per annum, or $3.35M for the initial 5-year term, based on the first year of the term.
D. THAT as the capital maintenance/renewal cost will be waived (see Financial Implications section in the Report dated April 7, 2026, entitled "Operator Appointment and Lease Approval for Social Facility at #300 - 485 West 42nd Avenue"), this constitutes an in-kind grant estimated at approximately $172,550 per annum, or $862,750 for the initial 5-year term, based on the first year of the term.
E. THAT no legal rights or obligations be created or arise by Council's adoption of the A to D above until the Lease has been executed by all parties.
F. THAT, pursuant to Section 206(1)(j) of the Vancouver Charter, Council deems the organization listed in A above that is not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency, to be contributing to the health and welfare of the City.
ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND B TO D  
BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11582)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new consultation rules for inclusionary zoning and density bonus
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved amendments to the Development Approval Procedure By-law. These changes add new consultation requirements for developers who use inclusionary zoning (rules that require affordable housing in new projects) or density bonus provisions (rules that let builders add extra floor space in exchange for community benefits). Developers must now follow the updated consultation steps set out in the April 9, 2026 staff report before their projects can be approved. The Director of Legal Services has been instructed to prepare the final bylaw for enactment.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the new consultation requirements in Appendix A of the April 9, 2026 report before starting any development application that involves inclusionary zoning or density bonus.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 text amendment for 61 rental units at 185-193 SW Marine Drive
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a text amendment to the CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) bylaw for 185-193 Southwest Marine Drive and 7980 Manitoba Street. The amendment increases the maximum floor space ratio (FSR) — the ratio of a building's total floor area to the size of the land it sits on — from 2.4 to 3.05, and the maximum building height from 17.4 metres (57 feet) to 20.4 metres (67 feet). This allows the development of a six-storey residential rental building with 61 units, of which 20% of the residential floor area will be secured as below-market rental units. The approval is subject to conditions, including the preparation of a Housing Agreement bylaw to ensure the below-market units remain affordable.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are the property owner or developer, review the conditions of approval in Appendix B of the report and prepare to meet them before the bylaw is enacted.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 25-storey tower and hotel at Cambie and 45th
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved a rezoning application for 6012-6088 Cambie Street. The property will be rezoned from RT-2 (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This allows the developer to build a 25-storey strata residential building and a 15-storey strata hotel on top of a three-storey podium. The project also includes space for a non-profit social services organization. The new zoning permits a much larger building — increasing the floor space ratio from 0.6 to 8.67 and the maximum height from 9.2 metres (30 feet) to 83 metres (272 feet). Council also approved changes to the Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law to match the new development.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 31-storey mixed-use tower at 5635-5655 Cambie St
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning application for 5635-5655 Cambie Street and 511 West 41st Avenue. The property will be rezoned from C-1 (Commercial) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development), which is a custom zoning district that allows more flexibility in building design and use. This change permits a 31-storey mixed-use building with 176 strata residential units, office space, a 49-space childcare facility, and ground-floor commercial uses. The maximum floor space ratio (FSR) — the ratio of a building's total floor area to the size of the land it sits on — increases from 1.2 to 9.7, and the maximum building height rises from 10.7 metres (35 feet) to 95.2 metres (312 feet). The decision is final and includes conditions that protect the City from any legal or financial obligations until the new zoning bylaw is formally enacted.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver council voted to block overdose prevention site at 900 Helmcken
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted to direct staff to use all available legal tools — including permits, licensing, and enforcement actions — to prevent the opening of an overdose prevention site at 900 Helmcken Street. The decision was made due to concerns from residents and businesses about public safety, neighbourhood conditions, and past problems with similar sites in the area. Council recognized the ongoing overdose crisis but decided the proposed location was not appropriate.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required from business owners — the decision is final and council has directed staff to block the site.
minutes_briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt
minutes_briefs.source_body:
2.  Stopping the Proposed Overdose Prevention Site at 900 Helmcken


* * * * *

Mayor Sim moved the above motion as urgent business.  

Following a brief recess, under section 9.6 (a) of the Procedure By-law, Acting Mayor Meiszner ruled that the motion qualifies as urgent business as it requires the urgent attention of Council in connection with public health or safety.

* * * * *


MOVED by Mayor Sim
SECONDED by Councillor Montague

WHEREAS  

1. Vancouver Coastal Health has advised of its intention to open an overdose prevention site at 900 Helmcken Street commencing June 1, 2026;  
2. The proposed site is located in a dense mixed-use area of downtown Vancouver, in proximity to residential buildings, seniors, local businesses, hotels, and significant pedestrian activity;  
3. Council has received concerns from residents and business operators regarding potential impacts on public safety, neighbourhood conditions, and business operations associated with the proposed site;  
4. Previous overdose prevention sites operating in the immediate area generated documented concerns related to public disorder, open drug use, and impacts on surrounding residents and businesses, and were subsequently closed;  
5. The proposed site is intended to be operated by the same non-profit organization responsible for prior sites in the area;  
6. Council recognizes the seriousness of the ongoing overdose crisis and the need for effective responses, including prevention, treatment, and recovery supports, alongside consideration of appropriate siting and community impacts; and  
7. The City of Vancouver has a responsibility to maintain public order, safety, and the reasonable enjoyment of neighbourhoods, and to take appropriate steps within its authority to address uses that may materially impact surrounding residents and businesses.  

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT Council direct staff to use all tools available to the City, including but not limited to permitting, licensing, servicing, and previous legal settlements, to take all lawful steps to prevent the opening of the proposed overdose prevention site at 900 Helmcken Street, including:  

i. A comprehensive review of all required permits, licences, and approvals to ensure strict compliance with City by-laws and regulations;
ii. Consideration of enforcement actions, including the refusal, suspension, or revocation of permits or business licences where legally supportable; and  
iii. Review of any prior agreements or settlements related to overdose prevention sites in the area, and to report back promptly to Council on available legal options and recommended actions.

carried

... (final motion as approved with same text)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 2026 MFABC tax rates for Vancouver property classes
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a resolution to substitute the Municipal Finance Authority of British Columbia (MFABC) tax rates for the 2026 taxation year. The MFABC is a provincial body that levies taxes on property to fund regional services. Council voted to keep the same tax rates as originally set by MFABC: 0.00020 per $1,000 of taxable value for Class 1 Residential, 0.00070 for Class 5 Light Industry, and 0.00050 for Class 6 Business & Other. This was done to adjust for changes in property values under the city's land assessment averaging bylaw, ensuring the total revenue raised stays the same. The resolution was carried unanimously.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council approved 2026 property tax rate bylaw
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council unanimously passed a bylaw to set property tax rates for 2026. The municipal general purpose tax levy increases 0.8% to $1.226 billion (0% Council-directed increase plus 0.8% from new construction). The 2026 averaged rates per $1,000 of taxable value are: Class 1 Residential $1.93406, Class 6 Business & Other $7.08019, Class 5 Light Industry $7.08019, Class 2 Utilities $37.47987, Class 4 Major Industry $33.41020. Other taxing authorities' rates are also set: Provincial School Tax (Class 6: 4.05900), TransLink (Class 6: 1.07093), BC Assessment (Class 6: 0.11353), Municipal Finance Authority (Class 6: 0.00050). The Development Potential Tax Relief By-law and 5-year land assessment averaging continue.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check your 2026 property assessment notice when it arrives and budget for the new tax rates.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved bylaw changes for drinking alcohol on city land
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has amended the City Land Regulation Bylaw (No. 8735) to designate 14 specific city-owned plazas and parklets where liquor may be consumed. The bylaw (No. 14672) takes effect May 10, 2026 and runs through May 9, 2029. Hours vary by area and season: most designated areas (2–11) permit consumption from 11:00 am to 9:00 pm during summer (May 10 to October 10) and 11:00 am to 5:00 pm during winter (October 10 to May 9). Area 1 (Drinker's Lounge Parklet) is open 9:30 am to 4:30 pm year-round. Area 12 (Kamloops & Hastings Neighbourhood Plaza) operates 5:00 pm to 9:00 pm in summer and 11:00 am to 5:00 pm in winter. Area 13 (Maple & 4th Neighbourhood Plaza) is summer-only, 11:00 am to 9:00 pm. Area 14 (Kamloops & Hastings Interim Neighbourhood Plaza) is summer-only, 5:00 pm to 9:00 pm, ending September 10, 2026.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the new Schedule 1 of the City Land Regulation Bylaw to confirm which designated areas and hours apply to your business location or event.

key_dates.label: Vancouver liquor consumption bylaw takes effect
Summer hours end — most designated areas switch to winter hours
Area 14 (Kamloops & Hastings Interim Plaza) season ends

minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a text amendment to the CD-1 (847) Comprehensive Development District bylaw for the property at 133-159 West 49th Avenue. The change gives property owner Alabaster Developments Ltd. permission to add more usable floor area to the commercial spaces without expanding the building's footprint.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new inclusionary zoning rules for Vancouver development
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted to replace the current density bonus system with new inclusionary zoning rules, effective June 30, 2026. This means developers will now be required to include affordable housing units in new projects instead of paying into a city fund. The change is part of the city's compliance with provincial Bill 16. Council also approved a plan to review and update the inclusionary zoning approach over the coming months. The new rules apply to the Downtown Official Development Plan and the Zoning and Development By-law.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are a developer or property owner in Vancouver's downtown area, review how the new inclusionary zoning rules will affect your upcoming projects before the June 30, 2026 effective date.

key_dates.label: New inclusionary zoning rules take effect — Vancouver
